narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, an LSAT score of 30, high family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 at the 4th decile and Year 3 at the 1st decile. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of past students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 52% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try—tipping just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction stems from weighing factors like grades, test scores, and background against patterns in students who succeeded or struggled on the bar. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking in the bottom tenth, which stood out as a strong signal of academic challenges compared to peers who passed, as it was well below the typical 5th or 6th decile average. Next, your high family income actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success by aligning with patterns of students from similar backgrounds who often had extra resources to excel. Your LSAT score of 30, lower than the average of about 37, also nudged things positively but less strongly, suggesting it helped somewhat despite not being a top score. Overall, your profile showed solid support from family resources and full-time commitment—common for 93% of students—but your later-year grades carried the heaviest weight in signaling risk. Compared to typical admitted students, your undergraduate GPA of 2.60 trails the 3.21 average, amplifying concerns from that low Year 3 decile. While this is disappointing, especially given your determination as a first-generation Hispanic applicant pushing through, it doesn't define your potential—the model just highlights patterns from past data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters emphasizing your resilience and community involvement. Many students in similar situations have improved these metrics and succeeded on appeal or at other programs. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback, and keep building your story—you've got the drive to rewrite this prediction.
